title: Regulating Act of 1773
provisions: The Governor of Bengal was designated as the Governor-General of Bengal.
The Governor-General was made responsible for all British territories in India, including Bombay and Madras.
To assist the Governor-General, an Executive Council was created, consisting of 4 executive members along with the Governor-General.
Decisions were to be taken by majority vote.
The Act called for the establishment of a Supreme Court in 1774 at Calcutta.
The Act banned private trade.
Company officials, upon returning home, were required to submit a detailed statement of the properties and wealth acquired in India.
Company officials were forbidden from accepting gifts or presents from the natives of India.
This Act was based on the report of a committee headed by British Prime Minister Lord North.
The governance of the East India Company was placed under the control of the British Parliament.
Warren Hastings was the first Governor-General under this Act.
The Governor-General of Bengal was empowered to make rules, regulations, and ordinances with the consent of the Supreme Court.

title: Pitt's India Act of 1784
provisions: This Act created a six-member Board of Control headed by the British Cabinet Minister, the Secretary of State.
The Board of Control was empowered to supervise the Company's civil, military, and revenue affairs.
The 24-member Court of Directors was placed under the surveillance of the Board of Control.
The Act initiated a system of joint rule by the British Parliament and the East India Company.
The Company was required to clearly distinguish between its commercial and political accounts.
Governor-General's Executive Council size 4->3
This Act was the first to describe the Company's territories in India as British possessions in India.
The Company was forbidden from intervening in the local political affairs of India.
The provinces were required to follow the instructions of the central government.
The Governor-General was empowered to dismiss a failing provincial government.

title: Special Act of 1786
provisions: This Act of the British Parliament gave the Governor-General overriding powers over the Executive Council.

title: Charter Act of 1793
provisions: This Act renewed the Company's charter for the next 20 years with all privileges intact.
The overriding powers previously granted to Cornwallis were extended to all future Governor-Generals.
Under this Act, the salaries of the Governor-General and the Executive Council were to be paid from Indian revenues rather than the British Exchequer.
The Act emphasized that the governance of India should be based on written laws rather than personal rule.
All written laws were to be translated into local languages so that people would be aware of their rights and immunities.
Courts were given the power to interpret rules and regulations.

title: Charter Act of 1813
provisions: This Act was influenced by the Continental System of Napoleon.
The Act renewed the Company's charter for the next 20 years.
It ended the Company's monopoly of trade with India.
The Company retained its monopoly over the tea trade and trade with China.
The Act stated that the objective of British authority in India was to establish British paramountcy.
Christian missionaries were allowed to go to India under a license to promote moral and religious education.
One lakh rupees were allocated from Indian revenues for the promotion of education in India.
Local autonomous bodies were empowered to levy taxes for the first time.

title: Charter Act of 1833
provisions: This Act renewed the Company's charter for the next 20 years and completely ended its trade monopoly.
The Company was asked to wind up its commercial operations in India.
The Company was allowed to retain and administer its political territories in India on behalf of His Majesty.
One legal expert (Legislative Member) was added to the Governor-General's Executive Council.
The Act led to the creation of the first Indian Law Commission under the chairmanship of Macaulay.
The Act stated that Indians would be employed in British administrative services and that their education would be ensured in public institutions without discrimination.
The Governor-General of Bengal was designated as the Governor-General of India.
The Governor-General of India was vested with full financial and administrative powers.
The Governor-General of India could repeal, amend, or alter laws and regulations with the approval of the Court of Directors.
A separate Presidency of Agra was to be created out of the Bengal Presidency.
The Act called for the upliftment of slaves in India.
While drafting laws related to Indian household and marriages, the rights of the heads of Indian families were to be taken into consideration.
The Council was granted full powers over revenue, and a single budget was to be prepared for the whole country.
The East India Company was reduced to an administrative and political entity, and several Lords and Ministers were nominated as ex-officio members of the Board of Control.
For the first time, the Governor-General's government was called the Government of India, and his Council was called the Indian Council.

title: Charter Act of 1853
provisions: Under this Act, the Company's charter was renewed without specifying any fixed timeline.
A separate Governor-General's Legislative Council was created with six new members.
The Act introduced the system of local representation in the Governor-General's Legislative Council.
Out of the six new members, four were to be appointed by the local governments of Bengal, Agra, Madras, and Bombay (with a condition of 10 years of service in the BEIC).
The Act reduced the number of members of the Court of Directors from 24 to 18.
Six members were to be nominated by the British Crown to the Court of Directors.
The Act stated that the Covenanted Civil Service would be opened to Indians, and the Macaulay Committee was constituted.
This Act followed the report of Governor-General Dalhousie on improving the Company's administration.
A separate Governor for Bengal was to be appointed.
The legislative and administrative functions of the Council were clearly separated.
Recruitment of the Company's employees was to be conducted through competitive examinations.

title: Government of India Act, 1858
provisions: This Act led to the complete transfer of powers from the BEIC to the British Crown.
The Governor-General of India was designated as the Viceroy of India.
Under this Act, the Governor-General became the administrative head of India, while the Viceroy acted as the representative of the British Crown.
The British Cabinet Minister, the Secretary of State, was made responsible for the Government of India.
To assist the Secretary of State, a 15-member Council of India was created.
This Act led to the abolition of the Board of Control and the Court of Directors.
The country was divided into provinces headed by a Governor or Lieutenant-Governor, aided by an Executive Council.
The provincial governments were required to function under the superintendence, direction, and control of the Governor-General in all matters.
